sections.about: This metric measures energy consumed per tonne of crude steel or physical output under BRSR Principle 6, Essential Indicator 1. It is the most relevant energy-efficiency metric in this set because it adjusts for scale. A lower value indicates better performance.
sections.performanceAndDrivers: Jindal Stainless leads in every comparable year, improving from 19.97 GJ/tcs in FY2024 to 16.36 GJ/tcs in FY2025. JSW is second, improving to 23.547 GJ/tcs in FY2025. SAIL improved from 32 GJ/tcs in FY2024 to 27 GJ/tcs in FY2025. Tata worsened from 24.5 GJ/tcs in FY2023 consolidated to 28.3 GJ/tcs in FY2024 and stayed at 28.3 GJ/tcs in FY2025. Tata’s BRSR energy-efficiency initiatives, including BEE collaboration, waste heat recovery and process gas recovery, are directionally positive, but they have not yet translated into peer-leading intensity. Jindal’s EAF liquid chrome optimisation, VFDs, waste heat recovery boiler and hot charging initiatives directly target electricity and fuel intensity.
sections.tataPositioning: In FY2025, Tata ranks 4th, with energy intensity 73.0% above Jindal Stainless. Tata’s rank was 3rd in FY2023 and FY2024, then declined to 4th in FY2025 as SAIL improved to 27 GJ/tcs. This is the most concerning Tata energy metric because it removes the “large producer” explanation and points to a direct efficiency gap.
sections.targets: Tata discloses energy-efficiency programmes across geographies and BEE collaboration. SAIL has a phased decarbonisation and PAT-linked efficiency roadmap. Jindal Stainless has PAT overachievement at Jajpur and Hisar and disclosed multiple FY2025 energy-saving initiatives.
sections.comparabilityNotes: SAIL FY2022-23 data is not available. Tata FY2022-23 is consolidated, reducing like-for-like comparability with later standalone years.

flags.flagNote: SAIL FY2023 physical-output intensity is missing, so the FY2023-24 SAIL trend cannot be assessed.
